coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric biosensor based on boronic acid-functionalized ZnO-derived nanostructures for the detection of N-acetylneuraminic acid and its in vivo bio-imaging studies
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers

coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description:
Background N-acetylneuraminic acid (Neu5Ac) is a sialic acid-based biomarker in neurological disorders like Alzheimer's disease (AD) and aging. Their abnormal concentrations in biological fluids like human serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are highly concerning. Conventional analytical methods lack time-saving, high sensitivity, and convenient labor options for their detection. In this study, a selectively recognizable fluorometric sensor was developed based on the boronic acid-functionalization of carbon-rich zinc oxide-derived nanostructures (ZnO NSs) for Neu5Ac detection.
Methods The ZnO NSs were synthesized using the acid-alkali-assisted hydrothermal method from anionic surfactants like sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). These were functionalized with 3-(aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES): (APT-ZnO NSs), aspirin [acetylsalicylic acid (ASA)]: (ASA-APT-ZnO NSs), and quercetin: (Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs). To enhance the detection capacity against Neu5Ac, Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs were modified with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) (APBA-ZnO NSs). Their structural, functional, and optical performance were investigated using XRD and Raman, FT-IR, and UV-Vis spectroscopy.
Significant findings Flower-like ZnO NSs showed a typical ZnO plane (110) of wurtzite hexagonal crystalline phase under HRTEM analysis. The novelty of the proposed work is the development of highly sensitive and efficient APBA-ZnO NSs for Neu5Ac detection in the linear concentration range of 0 to 8 µM with a low detection limit of 0.56 µM. These results are comparable to the other reports, and their multicolor fluorescence properties in zebrafish larvae confirm them as magnificent fluorescent probes for in vivo bio-imaging studies. The practicality of the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs was explored in serum and CSF samples, which showed potential recovery percentage ranges of 97–104.66% and 96.33–105.37%, respectively. In the future, these fluorescent probes will have the appropriate scope for the in vitro and in vivo detection of diverse neurological biomarkers.

head.ce:title: Fluorometric biosensor based on boronic acid-functionalized ZnO-derived nanostructures for the detection of N-acetylneuraminic acid and its in vivo bio-imaging studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-acetylneuraminic acid (Neu5Ac) is a sialic acid-based biomarker in neurological disorders like Alzheimer's disease (AD) and aging. Their abnormal concentrations in biological fluids like human serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are highly concerning. Conventional analytical methods lack time-saving, high sensitivity, and convenient labor options for their detection. In this study, a selectively recognizable fluorometric sensor was developed based on the boronic acid-functionalization of carbon-rich zinc oxide-derived nanostructures (ZnO NSs) for Neu5Ac detection.

The ZnO NSs were synthesized using the acid-alkali-assisted hydrothermal method from anionic surfactants like sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). These were functionalized with 3-(aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES): (APT-ZnO NSs), aspirin [acetylsalicylic acid (ASA)]: (ASA-APT-ZnO NSs), and quercetin: (Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs). To enhance the detection capacity against Neu5Ac, Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs were modified with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) (APBA-ZnO NSs). Their structural, functional, and optical performance were investigated using XRD and Raman, FT-IR, and UV-Vis spectroscopy.

Flower-like ZnO NSs showed a typical ZnO plane (110) of wurtzite hexagonal crystalline phase under HRTEM analysis. The novelty of the proposed work is the development of highly sensitive and efficient APBA-ZnO NSs for Neu5Ac detection in the linear concentration range of 0 to 8 µM with a low detection limit of 0.56 µM. These results are comparable to the other reports, and their multicolor fluorescence properties in zebrafish larvae confirm them as magnificent fluorescent probes for in vivo bio-imaging studies. The practicality of the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs was explored in serum and CSF samples, which showed potential recovery percentage ranges of 97–104.66% and 96.33–105.37%, respectively. In the future, these fluorescent probes will have the appropriate scope for the in vitro and in vivo detection of diverse neurological biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: N-acetylneuraminic acid (Neu5Ac) is a potent derivative of sialic acid (SA), which is predominantly available in the form of nine-carbon acidic sugar bound to glycoconjugates like glycolipids and glycoproteins over the cell surfaces [1]. However, another derivative of SA has existed in nature that is N-glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc), which is present in many mammals except humans [2]. Coming to the Neu5Ac, its excessive concentrations were already found in the human brain, which mediates typical functions such as neural plasticity, neuronal sprouting, and myelin stability [3]. More importantly, Neu5Ac is acting as a valid neurological biomarker and its metabolism is significantly affecting the neurogenesis by impairing neuronal networks leading to the amyloidogenesis, which finally causes Alzheimer's disease (AD) [4]. Also, the overexpression of Neu5Ac is widely linked to tumorigenesis by progressing tumor proliferation, and metastasis, and by resisting apoptosis [5]. Noteworthy, the alterations in Neu5Ac metabolism led to insulin resistance in the AD patients, which simultaneously increased sugar levels in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) [6]. Again, elevated concentrations of Neu5Ac were reported in the serum of AD patients, which served as a biomarker [7]. In this regard, the determination of Neu5Ac in biological fluids like human CSF and serum is highly necessary for understanding critical neurological disorders like AD.

Previously, several works were dedicated to the detection and evaluation of the Neu5Ac. For instance, Monteiro and his co-workers synthesized 3-mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA)-modified quantum dots (QDs) for identifying Neu5Ac on the red blood cells (RBCs) [8]. The boronic acid-mediated sensors are very crucial in developing favorable detecting strategies against Neu5Ac sensing. In this context, 3-pyridylboronic acid-functionalized CDs were developed for the detection of Neu5Ac with a detection limit of 54 µM [9]. The other research group prepared boron (B) and nitrogen (N)-doped near-infrared (NIR)-fluorescent CDs using 3-APBA and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as precursors for the Neu5Ac sensing with a detection limit of 9.24 µM [10]. The colorimetric sensors based on APBA-modified Au NPs have shown adequate sensing abilities against Neu5Ac with detection limits around 60 and 35 µM, respectively [11,12]. Sankoh et al. developed the colorimetric approach for Neu5Ac sensing using 4-MPBA-functionalized Au NPs with a detection limit of 68 ± 2 µM [13]. Nevertheless, these reports are lacking a high sensitivity against Neu5Ac and have not demonstrated in vivo bio-imaging potentials. In this context, potential nanomaterials designed with highly selective recognizable groups (e.g., boronic acid) are necessary, which can detect Neu5Ac with significantly substantial detection limits. The present study has addressed above drawbacks in terms of both sensitivity and bio-imaging using proposed ZnO-derived nanostructures, which is required for the better understanding of the Neu5Ac detection.

ZnO-related nanomaterials have been employed in diverse applications including bioimaging, photoluminescence (PL), photocatalysis, electrochemical, gas sensing, optoelectronics, and antibacterial applications [14,15]. So far, a plethora of ZnO-derived nanostructures (ZnO NSs) has been developed such as nanoparticles (NPs), nanowires (NWs), nanorods (NRs), nanoflowers (NFs), and nanosheets. These nanomaterials are highly desirable due to their characteristic features such as superior biocompatibility, huge surface area, enormous photochemical, and thermal stability with a wide bandgap (3.37 eV) and a high exciton binding energy (60 meV) at room temperature. For instance, ZnO NPs have been widely adopted for multifaceted potentials in biomedical fields including drug delivery, biosensors, and tissue engineering [16–18]. Over the last decade, ZnO nanomaterials can be employed in bioimaging [19,20]. Previously carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and cerium oxide NPs were reported the angiogenic features [21,22]. Barui et al. developed ZnO NFs, which exhibited angiogenic properties by forming new capillaries in both in vitro and in vivo models [23]. Recently, ZnO NFs by utilizing these angiogenic potentials have been employed for treating peripheral artery diseases (PADs) like critical limb ischemia (CLI) [24]. In another study, Patra and co-workers have studied the neuritogenic performance of ZnO NFs which demonstrated enhanced neurite outgrowths both in vitro (Neuro 2a cells) and in vivo (zebrafish embryos) [25].

Till now, multiple synthesis routes have been reported for preparing ZnO NSs including hydrothermal, sonochemical, reflux, oxidation, and microwave reactions [26–34]. Though each technique has its own merits and limitations, the hydrothermal method has been widely reported for the preparation of a myriad of ZnO NSs [35–37]. For example, Katiyar et al. reported ZnO NFs through the ultrasonic-supported hydrothermal method without using any substrates at a low temperature (95 °C) for 4 h [38]. Umar et al. prepared ZnO nanourchins by hydrothermal reaction using conditions around 165 °C and 5 h [39]. Xu and co-workers designed ZnO NFs by employing a hydrothermal approach at 120 °C and 8 h [40]. In another study, Ameen et al. developed cabbage-like ZnO NSs using hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for ∼12 h [41].

In the last decade, biogenic synthesis has gained great demand for the synthesis of ZnO NFs using a variety of natural precursors. For instance, Tripathi et al. developed ZnO NFs using the Bacillus licheniformis (a non-pathogenic bacteria) by incubating with Zinc precursors under continuous shaking (∼200 rpm) at 37 ± 1 °C for 48 h [42]. In another study, Din et al. prepared ZnO NFs by employing eco-friendly maltose as both capping and reducing agents [43]. On the other hand, ZnO-nanocomposites have also played a significant role in divergent fields. Chakraborty et al. developed ZnO–Ag2O composite NFs on the gold (Au) substrate for electrochemical sensing of 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT) [44]. Zhang and their co-workers have synthesized ZnO NFs by thermal decomposition strategy and made them into ZnO-reduced graphene oxide (RGO) nanocomposites which are employed in gas sensing and photocatalysis applications [45]. Recently, Shafai et al. developed nanocomposites based on GO fabricated with Ag, TiO2, and ZnO NFs (GO–Ag–TiO2@ZnO) for antibacterial applications [46].

In this study, we synthesized ZnO-derived NSs using an acid-alkali-based hydrothermal method without calcination. Further, these ZnO NSs were modified using carbon-rich ligands such as APTES, aspirin i.e., acetylsalicylic acid (ASA), and quercetin to increase their carbon content and bio-compatibility. The resulting ZnO-NSs were modified with APBA ligand (APBA-ZnO NSs) to enhance their sensitivity for the detection of Neu5Ac. Their PL properties are highly encouraging for the investigation of bioimaging applications in animal (zebrafish) models. To investigate the efficiency of the APBA-ZnO NSs on human, firstly, we selected zebrafish larvae as biological models. For this, bio-imaging ability of the APBA-ZnO NSs was studied under confocal microscopy, which distinctly visualizes the zebrafish larvae in multiple color emissions ranging from blue to red. These multi-color imaging properties are arising due to their robust optical behavior resulting from conjugated aromatic structures. Next, the practicality of APBA-ZnO NSs was investigated against Neu5Ac in biological samples such as human serum and CSF, which signify their suitable sensitivity in the physiological system.

Zinc oxide (ZnO), 3-(aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES), aspirin i.e., acetylsalicylic acid (ASA), quercetin, N-acetylneuraminic acid (Neu5Ac), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), calcium hydroxide Ca (OH)2, magnesium hydroxide Mg (OH)2, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), glucose, fructose, galactose, mannose, sucrose, lactose, dopamine hydrochloride, epinephrine, serotonin (5-HT), l-glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (L-Cys), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC hydrochloride), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Glacial acetic acid, hydrochloric acid (HCl), and ethanol were obtained from Duksan, South Korea. Deionized water was utilized for all experiments. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 5.5) was used for the sensing applications.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) studies were investigated using Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4700, HITACHI, Japan) with an accelerating voltage (20 kV) was used. XPS spectra were measured using the Thermo Scientific K-AlphaTM X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer with a range of motion (100–4000 eV), 128-channel detector, and a double-focusing hemispherical analyzer (180°) with a micro-focused X-ray source (Al Kα). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were processed by Rigaku ultima III X-ray diffractometer equipped with Cu sealed tube of λ = 1.54,178 Å), 40 kV, 30 mA, the scan speed of 2°/min in continuous scanning 2θ mode. Raman spectra were recorded by micro-Raman spectroscopy (ANDOR Monora500i, 633 nm). Thermal stability studies were carried out through the SDT Q600 (V20.9 Build 20) (TGA-DTA) analyzer under N2 with heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and a flow rate of 100 mL min−1. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using the Varian Cary 100 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements were performed by Quanta Master, Photon Technology International (PTI), NJ, USA, equipped with a Xe lamp of Arc Lamp Housing, A-1010BTM, a monochromator, and the power supply (Brytexbox). Fluorescence lifetime decay data was acquired by the PTI EasyLife II fluorometer system containing auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths (260–650 nm). EasyLife II software consisting of the RS232 and the USB connection was employed to obtain the curve fitting data. The pH was measured by the Thermo Scientific pH meter. Confocal images of zebrafish larvae were procured via laser scanning confocal microscope (Nikon eclipse TE 2000-U).

ZnO NSs were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal approach by modifying the previous method [47]. Before starting the reaction, the two glass slides were cleaned with aqua regia (HCl and HNO3; 3:1, v/v) and were arranged into the Teflon autoclave in an upright position for the better surface attachment of ZnO NSs. In a typical reaction, ZnO (1.5 g) was dissolved in a beaker containing each 20 mL of DI water and GAA. This mixture was continuously stirred for 15 min at ambient conditions and kept aside. To the above mixture, a 5 mL of SDS solution prepared in DI water and a 40 mL of concentrated HCl (6 M) were added carefully and thoroughly stirred. While stirring ethanol (5 mL), and NaOH (1 mL) was added dropwise to this reaction mixture and allowed to stir for 30 min at room temperature. After the appearance of the pale-yellow colored solution in the beaker, the reaction mixture was taken out and placed in the above-mentioned autoclave and the synthesis was performed in a hot air oven at 150 °C for 6 h. After the completion of the reaction, the solution present in the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. All the glass slides were carefully removed and gently scratched for collecting the powder. The powder was thoroughly rinsed with a mixture of DI water and ethanol and centrifuged (6000 rpm, for 20 min) and further air-dried under an oven at 110 °C. Furthermore, the functionalization of ZnO NSs was carried out using carbon-rich ligands such as APTES (APT-ZnO NSs), aspirin (ASA-APT-ZnO NSs), and quercetin (Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs) to prepare carbon-rich ZnO NSs. These experimental details were incorporated into the supplementary information.

Firstly, 20 mg of EDC was added to the 20 mL (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4) of Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs and shaken for 30 min. Next, 13 mg of NHS was added to the above solution and stirred for 2 h under dark conditions at room temperature. Then, a 15 mg of APBA was added in to the above reaction mixture. The mixture was incubated for 24 h with continuous magnetic stirring under the same ambient conditions. The obtained APBA-Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs can be termed shortly as APBA-ZnO NSs, which were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. Finally, these were dialyzed against DI water through a cellulose ester membrane (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 48 h to remove unreacted APBA moieties. Finally, these ZnO NSs were dried under hot air oven and refrigerated for other studies. The dried solutions of native ZnO NSs and functionalized APBA-ZnO NSs are appeared in the form of white powders (Fig. S1). The synthesis and functionalization steps for the ZnO NSs are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The quantum yield for the starting ZnO NSs and final APBA-ZnO NSs were calculated using the quinine sulfate: 0.1 M H2SO4 as the standard solution by the Fluoro-Q2100 quantum yield instrument. The following equation was employed for measuring the quantum yield.
(1)
Quantum Yield
= Ec / ( La − Lc )

The Ec, La, and Lc are the emission by the direct excitation light source, cumulative quantity of excitation light, and the total amount of light measured immediately after the direct excitation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of Neu5Ac through APBA-ZnO NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The APBA-ZnO NSs were applied for the detection of Neu5Ac via fluorometric approach. A 100 μL of APBA-ZnO NSs (20 mg/mL) was added to the cuvette consisting a 2 mL of PBS (10 mM, pH 5.5). Then, various concentrations of Neu5Ac (0–8 μM) were mixed to the above mixture and subsequently incubated for 30 min. Finally, PL spectra were recorded by employing the PL spectrophotometer at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging studies in zebrafish larvae using APBA-ZnO NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The zebrafish larvae (ck112a) were acquired from the Zebrafish Center for Disease Modeling (ZCDM), Chungnam National University, South Korea. For bio-imaging studies, the zebrafish larvae were incubated for 3 h in a six-well plate consisting of APBA-ZnO NSs. Subsequently, after the incubation, the excess solutions of APBA-ZnO NSs were removed gently using butter paper and the zebrafish larvae were positioned over a cover slip on the glass slide and the imaging was investigated under confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Neu5Ac in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the APBA-ZnO NSs was investigated in biological fluids including human serum and CSF through the determination of Neu5Ac. These samples were thoroughly centrifuged (9000 rpm; 10 min), and resulting supernatants were kept aside. A 0.5 mL of acetonitrile was mixed to these samples to get remained precipitated proteins. Next, the supernatant solutions were centrifuged (9000 rpm; 10 min). The samples were 50-fold diluted using PBS (10 mM, pH 5.5). The final spiked samples were prepared by adding multiple concentrations of Neu5Ac (1, 3, 5, 6, and 8 μM). The obtained spiked samples were used for the determination of Neu5Ac by the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs.

The morphology of ZnO NSs was investigated using SEM, TEM, and XRD techniques. The microscopic investigations for the ZnO NSs were investigated under SEM analysis. The petals are similar to the nanosheets, which assembled to form the flower-shaped structures with the average size around 4 μm (Fig. 1 A–D).

These morphological characteristics were attributed from the addition of anionic surfactant i.e., SDS, which signifies the well-aggregation of the nanosheets or flower-petals during the hydrothermal synthesis [48].

Fig. 2 A–F shows the TEM and HRTEM images of ZnO NSs with the emergence of flower like NSs at different magnifications. A distinguishably two different portions of the NSs were investigated to measure the size of petals, which show the minimum and maximum size of 9 and 25 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). The HRTEM results show the interplanar distance of 0.281 nm corresponding to the ZnO (110) lattice plane [49]. The inset HRTEM image shows the selected area diffraction (SAED) and Fast Fourier transformation (FFT) patterns for the ZnO NSs, which is consistent with the hexagonal wurtzite crystalline structure (Fig. 2F).

The powder XRD analysis of ZnO NSs shows the multiple sharp peaks with diffraction planes of (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), and (202) at 30.01°, 31.29°, 35.10°, 48.25°, 54.65°, 62.41°, 65.97°, 67.44°, 72. 79°, and 75.84°, respectively (Fig. 3 A). These planes are suggesting the hexagonal structure of ZnO in the ZnO NSs [50,51]. The other sharp diffraction planes of (003) and (006) were observed in the XRD pattern of ZnO NSs at 6.82° and 8.29°, respectively, which attributed to the incorporation of SDS [52,53]. Also, numerous distinguished sharp peaks were denoted by star (*) symbol were noticed at 16.64°, 18.6°, 21.4°, 23.06°, 25.09°, and 27.24°, which are responsible for the crystallinity of SDS (Fig. S6A) [54]. These XRD results are consistent with the HRTEM results, which confirms the hexagonal wurtzite crystalline phases of ZnO [55].

Raman analysis for the ZnO NSs was investigated to understand their graphitization and structural defects (Fig. 3B). The typical ZnO peak in the ZnO NSs was observed at 413 cm−1, which is responsible for the wurtzite ZnO E2 high mode. Generally, the Raman spectra of native ZnO nanomaterials have shown the E2 high mode around 430 cm−1 [56]. Whereas the resultant blue-shift might have observed from the defects or internal strains during the hydrothermal synthesis of ZnO NSs. A small peak in the ZnO NSs at 301 cm−1 is denoted E2 high - E2 low phonons. The broad and slightly suppressed peaks were observed at 615 cm−1, which can be belong to the A1 (LO) vibration mode, responsible for the availability of oxygen vacancies [56]. The peaks at 112, 1057, and 1101 cm−1, representing the E2 low phonon, A1(TO)+E1(TO)+E2L and the combined A1 and E2 modes in ZnO NSs [57]. The typical D and G bands were observed at 1373 and 1597 cm−1, which are responsible for sp3 and sp2 carbon bonding, respectively [58,59]. These are resulting from the carbon-containing precursors (SDS, ethanol, and GAA) in the ZnO NSs. These results are in good agreement with the HRTEM results and signifying the crystalline nature ZnO NSs.

The TGA analysis was carried out for the ZnO NSs at varied temperatures. From these results, the weight loss of 28.18% was observed, which obtained due to the decomposition of hydroxyl (-OH), carbonyl (CO), and sulfate (SO4 2−) groups (Fig. 3C). The first 20% of weight loss was observed in the TGA plot of ZnO NSs within the temperature of 200 °C. Only 8.18% of the weight loss was observed in the ZnO NSs between 200 and 700 °C. It suggests that ZnO NSs are thermally stable materials and are consistent with the previously reported ZnO NSs [60,61].

To investigate the elemental composition of ZnO NSs, we employed energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) equipped scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. S9A–F shows the elemental mapping results for ZnO NSs, which demonstrate zinc (Zn), carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na), sulphur (S), and chlorine (Cl). These results were confirmed through the corresponding EDX spectrum (Fig. S9G). Further, the EDX spectrum of ZnO NSs was revealed the weight and atomic percentages of the carbon around 34.45% and 54.79%, respectively.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was investigated to study the chemical environment of the ZnO NSs (Fig. 3D). The FT-IR spectra of ZnO NSs show characteristic stretching and deformation vibrations of ZnO at 414, 504, 602, 661, 752, and 1636 cm−1, respectively. The symmetric and asymmetric modes of CO were observed at 2337 and 2356 cm−1. The OH deformation and stretching vibrations were observed at 1124, and 3297, 3410, 3502, and 3604 cm−1, respectively. These results are suggesting that ZnO NSs are composed of ZnO chemical bonding.

The elemental environments of the ZnO NSs were studied using XPS technique. ZnO NSs were majorly comprised of C 1 s, Zn 2p, O 1 s, S 2p, Cl 2p, and Na 1 s. The total survey spectrum shows the above-mentioned elements around 285.08, 1023.08, 532.08, 169.08, 200.08, and 1072.08 eV, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s showed a peak at 284.39 eV attributed to CC (sp3) (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C shows the Zn 2p high-resolution spectra of ZnO NSs, which displayed deconvolutions at 1023.08 and 1046. 17 eV assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. These strong peaks are obtained from the direct inclusion of ZnO. The O 1 s high-resolution spectra of ZnO NSs, which exhibited deconvolutions around 532.10 and 535.03 eV ascribed to Zn-O/CO and Zn-OH/COH, respectively (Fig. 4D) [62]. These groups were examined in the FT-IR spectra of ZnO NSs, which correlate with the O 1 s spectra from XPS studies. The high-resolution spectra of S 2p of ZnO NSs show two peaks at 169.05 and 170.31 eV, which ascribed to sulfate groups of SDS moiety (Fig. 4E). The high-resolution Cl 2p spectra shows two peaks at 199.12 and 200.72 eV, which correspond to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively as shown in Fig. 4F. These two peaks are attributed to the addition of HCl for preparing ZnO NSs. The high-resolution spectrum of Na 1 s shows a peak around 1072.38 eV (Fig. 4G). The existence of this peak was ascribed to the addition of NaOH during the synthesis of ZnO NSs.

The optical properties of the as-prepared ZnO NSs, APT-ZnO NSs, and Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs were studied by employing UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy methods. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the ZnO NSs have shown two absorption bands at 280 and 349 nm. The band at 349 nm is a characteristic absorption for the ZnO NSs (black curve, Fig. 5 A) [63]. The band at 280 nm possibly represents the π-π* transition (sp2 carbons), which is attributed to the formation of ZnO NSs during the hydrothermal reaction. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of pure APTES showed a single absorption peak at 214 nm (Fig. S17A). However, after the functionalization of these ZnO NSs with the APTES (i.e., APT-ZnO NSs), a slight red shift in the absorption band to 295 nm with a typical ZnO band around 349 nm was observed (blue curve, Fig. 5A). A declined intensity in the absorption was observed in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of APT-ZnO NSs, which suggests the functionalization of ZnO NSs by APTES [64]. Further, we measured the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of ASA alone, which showed π-π* and n-π* transitions at 239 and 275 nm, respectively (Fig. S17B). These transitions are corresponded to the CC and CO moieties, respectively. As a result, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of ASA-APT-ZnO NSs displayed slight shifting in their bands to 232 and 296 nm, respectively (Fig. S17C). Also, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of quercetin alone has shown two typical absorption bands at 253 and 368 nm (Fig. S18A). Noteworthy, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs showed two distinguishable absorption peaks at 256 and 376 nm, which are attributed to the strong conjugation between quercetin and ZnO (orange curve, Fig. 5A) [65]. The inset images are showing the blue and orange fluorescence for the ZnO NSs, APT-ZnO NSs, and Qu-ASA-APT-ZnO NSs, respectively under the UV- illumination (i–iii, Fig. 5A). Further, to understand the optical properties in detail, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of pure APBA was investigated along with that of APBA-ZnO NSs (Fig. S18B). The pure APBA has revealed two main UV-Vis absorption bands at 232 and 295 nm, which represent the π-π* and n-π* transitions resulting from the (CC) to (CO) moieties, respectively. Interestingly, in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of APBA-ZnO NSs, the first absorption band was red-shifted to 241 nm, whereas a reduction in the second absorption band was also observed (brown circle) (Fig. S18C). It suggests that some of the boronic acid groups were interacted with the final APBA-ZnO NSs. Additionally, to further confirm the mechanism of detection, we added the Neu5Ac (8 µM) to the final APBA-ZnO NSs. Where the first absorption band was further red-shifted to 243 nm with a complete disappearance in the second absorption band, which suggests the sensitivity of APBA-ZnO NSs towards the Neu5Ac (Fig. S18D).

The ZnO NSs have shown the PL excitation (black curve) and emission maxima (red curve) around 358 and 460 nm as illustrated in Fig. 5B. More importantly, the excitation-dependent-emission behavior was observed in the PL emission spectra under varied excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm with increments of 20 nm as shown in Fig. 5C. This behavior is responsible for the existence of divergent surface emissive groups over the surface of ZnO NSs [66]. Also, these results are significantly corroborating with the previously reported ZnO nano colloids, ZnO granular films, and ZnO-associated CDs [67,68]. Further, the final APBA-ZnO NSs have shown a PL emission maximum of 461 nm (blue curve) under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm (black curve) with a strong-blue fluorescence (inset image) in DI water (Fig. 5D). Further, the optical properties of APTES and ASA alone, and their combination (ASA-APT-ZnO NSs) were studied in detail. The APT-ZnO NSs have demonstrated the PL excitation (black curve) and emission peak maxima (blue curve) with 339 and 436 nm, respectively (Fig. S19). The slight changes in the optical properties were observed, which probably have resulted from the APTES-modification over the ZnO NSs. Additionally, the fluorescence behavior of ASA-APT-ZnO NSs was investigated in DI water, which displayed the PL excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) maxima of 339 and 434 nm, respectively (Fig. S20). The inset image shows their moderately strong-blue color emission under the UV-illumination. It shows that the functionalization of APT-ZnO NSs with ASA does not much alternate the fluorescence behavior of ASA-APT-ZnO NSs. Finally, the ZnO NSs and APBA-ZnO NSs showed quantum yields of 6% and 19%, respectively, which attributed to the difference in their recombination dynamics [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of APBA-ZnO NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the APBA-ZnO NSs was studied against various concentrations of Neu5Ac. Fig. 6 A shows the fluorescence quenching of the APBA-ZnO NSs with different concentrations of Neu5Ac ranging from 0 to 8 µM. A linear regression plot was drawn between (F0-F)/F0) and Neu5Ac concentrations (0–8 µM), which achieved a correlation coefficient around R2 = 0.9958 (Fig. 6B). Herein, F0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensity of APBA-ZnO NSs before and after the addition of Neu5Ac. An adequate limit of detection (LOD) for Neu5Ac was found i.e., 0.56 µM, according to the equation 3σ/k. The σ and k are the standard deviation and slope of the regression curve. The expressions of ganglioside SA in the adult rat brains are generally found to be 3.9 µM/g fresh weight, which signifies that the proposed ZnO NSs are highly sensitive to detecting the Neu5Ac [70]. To investigate the selectivity of the APBA-ZnO NSs against Neu5Ac, we studied the PL response using over the other competent interferants. For this, we have chosen significant metal ions (Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, and K+), monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, mannose, and galactose), disaccharides (sucrose and lactose), and other neurotransmitters containing cis-diols (dopamine and epinephrine). The PL intensity was measured using higher concentrations (20 µM) of interferants against Neu5Ac (8 µM), where the APBA-ZnO NSs have shown more fluorescence quenching (Ca. 40%) towards Neu5Ac compared to that of other interferants as shown in Fig. 6C. The associated UV-illuminated photographs of APBA-ZnO NSs treated with Neu5Ac and other interferants are illustrated in Fig. 6D. From the above observations, the PL intensity of the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs has not responded to structurally similar interferants like saccharides and cis-diols. The main reason for the high selectivity of the developed APBA-ZnO NSs towards Neu5Ac is the pH 5.5. At this pH, the boronic acid moieties are available in the trigonal form. Hence, they can readily interact with the triol side chain of the Neu5Ac. In contrast, at higher pH, the boronic acid groups are exist in the tetrahedral form, which facilitate the binding with sugars like glucose. Thus, it is suggesting that the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs are highly favorable for the selective detection of Neu5Ac at pH 5.5.

From these results, the reasonable fluorescence quenching has occurred in APBA-ZnO NSs during the treatment of Neu5Ac. Again, we have tested the selectivity of these ZnO NSs using Neu5Ac (8 µM) in the presence of other biological molecules (30 µM) such as 5-HT, L-GSH, L-Cys, L-Glu, UA, and AA by investigating fluorescence studies (Fig. S21). The APBA-ZnO NSs have demonstrated a potentially high PL quenching in terms of intensity in contrast to the above-mentioned interferants. Again, these results are also highlighting the adequate selectivity and sensitivity of APBA-ZnO NSs against Neu5Ac among biologically relevant interferants [71]. To the best of our knowledge, there were no studies were reported utilizing 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA)-functionalized ZnO-derived NSs for the detection of Neu5Ac. Nevertheless, previously developed sensors have not shown a high sensitivity against Neu5Ac. Table 1 summarizes the comparison for the currently developed APBA-ZnO NSs to the formerly reported fluorescence [9,10], colorimetric [11–13], and electrochemical-based sensors [72,73]. This signifies that the proposed biosensor delivers a significant sensing potential for the detection of Neu5Ac. The obtained results are signifying the necessity of the combination of highly sensitive and cost-effective biosensors. Hence, the proposed senor with state-of-the-art potentials can conveniently deal with the clinical diagnosis of aging or other complicated neurodegenerative disorders.

The stability of the prepared APBA-ZnO NSs was studied under different environments including oxidizing (H2O2), pH, and ionic solutions (NaCl), and at different time intervals. The pH stability of APBA-ZnO NSs was analyzed by using various pH values ranging from 4.5 to 6.75 (Fig. S22A). They showed a little increase in the PL intensity after pH values around 4.5, which represents their fluorescence stability in acidic environments. Around pH 5.5, the maximum PL intensity was observed in the APBA-ZnO NSs, which is ideal for investigating critical diseases like brain tumors or other neurological disorders. After pH 5.5, their PL intensity slightly declined because of the deprotonation of boronic moieties from the APBA-ZnO NSs in the slightly acidic and alkaline conditions. Further, APBA-ZnO NSs have exhibited adequate stability with similar fluorescence intensity under NaCl solutions (0–1.0 mol L−1) (Fig. S22B). These results are suggesting their reasonable fluorescence stability, which are corroborating with the fluorescence stability of orange-red fluorescent zinc sulphide (ZnS) QDs [74]. The APBA-ZnO NSs have delivered a reasonable resistance without loss of much PL intensity when investigated in H2O2 at millimolar concentrations (0–1.0 mM L−1) as shown in Fig. S22C. This behavior is signifying that these ZnO NSs are highly suitable in oxidizing conditions to apply as chemosensors [75]. The PL intensity of the APBA-ZnO NSs was studied at different time intervals (every 25 days) from 0 to 150 days, which showed the uniform PL intensity without causing fluorescence quenching and suggesting their adequate fluorescence property under proper storage (Fig. S22D). The obtained excellent fluorescence stability of APBA-ZnO NSs against H2O2 and NaCl is beneficial for exploring biological studies. It is highlighting that the proposed sensor is beneficial in studying the crucial physiological systems in the human, which are similarly mimicked.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible PL quenching mechanism of APBA-ZnO NSs for Neu5Ac sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the boronic acid moieties present in the APBA-ZnO NSs can be interchangeable from trigonal to tetrahedral configuration depending on the pH of the sensor. Interestingly, the higher pH of the proposed sensor is not suitable for the detection of Neu5Ac due to the greater electrostatic repulsions exerted by the Neu5Ac towards the tetrahedral boronic acid. On contrary, the pH 5.5 is more convenient for the specific recognition and selective binding of trigonal boronic acids to the triol chains of Neu5Ac. Therefore, the other saccharides and cis-diol-containing compounds cannot be detected at pH 5.5 and thereby, the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs have shown a highly selective fluorometric response for Neu5Ac. The mechanism for the plausible fluorescence quenching of APBA-ZnO NSs by Neu5Ac is illustrated in Fig. 7 .

The boronic acid groups available in APBA-ZnO NSs readily interact with the free triol chains of Neu5Ac at pH 5.5, which leads to the formation of boronic acid-Neu5Ac complexes. This triggers the electron transfer between the non-complex form of APBA-ZnO NSs and the complex form of APBA-ZnO NSs consisting of boronic acid and Neu5Ac. This probably led to the fluorescence quenching in the APBA-ZnO NSs. Further, the above mechanism was confirmed with the lifetime decay studies. For this, lifetime decay analysis for the APBA-ZnO NSs was carried out against Neu5Ac by measuring their fluorescence quenching as shown in Fig. S23. The decay curves were plotted by double exponential fitting through the given equation:

The D, τi, and ai are the fluorescence decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential parameters. The APBA-ZnO NSs alone showed the lifetimes τ1 of 9.76 ns and τ2 of 0.021 ns. Whereas, the lifetimes of APBA-ZnO NSs with the addition of Neu5Ac, have exhibited remarkable decline with τ1 of 7.94 ns and τ2 of 0.025 ns. It suggests that the possible fluorescence quenching may have been ascribed to the dynamic fluorescence quenching through the electron transfer between APBA-ZnO NSs alone and the boronic acid-Neu5Ac complex formed in the APBA-ZnO NSs.

The morphology of the ZnO NSs was analyzed during the early drying period using the optical microscope, which revealed the distinguished flower formation (Fig. S24). Further, the fluorescence microscopic observations have shown their multi-color imaging response during the process of drying (Fig. S25). The ZnO NSs have demonstrated multi-color imaging behavior with blue, green, and red colored emissions under different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 543 nm, respectively. To investigate the applicability of the APBA-ZnO NSs as a fluorescent bioprobe for in vivo models (zebrafish), the zebrafish larvae were incubated with APBA-ZnO NSs for 3 h. Subsequently, their bio-imaging properties were observed under a confocal microscope with different excitation wavelengths including 405, 488, and 543 nm (Fig. 8 ). The resulting multicolor imaging is beneficial i.e., blue, green, and red-colored emissions in the larvae suggesting that the proposed APBA-ZnO NSs are highly indispensable nanomaterials for bio-imaging studies. Again, these results are highlighting the adequate distribution and uptake of APBA-ZnO NSs in the biological models, which demonstrate their excellent PL emission properties. Finally, these studies are addressing the promising features of the novel fluorescent ZnO NSs for investigating biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Neu5Ac in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The specific determination of sialic acids like Neu5Ac in the biological fluids (human serum and CSF) is helpful in the diagnosis of many pathological phenomena. These results can provide an indispensable potential for the early diagnosis of age-related diseases in neurologically complicated individuals. The proposed APBA-ZnO NSs are advantageous as biosensors for the detection of Neu5Ac in complex biological fluids i.e., human serum and CSF. The spiked samples were prepared using Neu5Ac at distinguished concentrations (1, 3, 5, 6, and 8 μM) in both the biological samples. Tables 2 and 3 show the spiking recovery values for the spiked serum and CSF samples of Neu5Ac, which include 97–104.66% and 96.33–105.37%, respectively. The values of the relative standard deviation (RSD) in both the samples are including 1.28–5.62% and 1.51–5.76%, respectively. These results are highly signifying their feasibility for the detection of Neu5Ac in biological samples. The investigated sensing abilities of APBA-ZnO NSs in human serum and CSF are helpful in finding the proper diagnosis of neurological disorders, where substantially fluctuated Neu5Ac levels are observed.

In this study, we synthesized blue-fluorescent ZnO NSs using surfactant and acid-alkali mediated hydrothermal reaction without calcination. Further, the modified ZnO NSs with high-carbonaceous ligands (APTES, aspirin, and quercetin) are more biocompatible. The final APBA-functionalized ZnO NSs are highly convenient in achieving high sensitivity toward Neu5Ac with a LOD of 0.56 µM in the concentration range of 0–8 µM. In general, the expressions Neu5Ac in mammals like the brains of rats are quantified in micromolar levels however, the obtained high sensitivity is more accountable for measuring ultra-micromolar concentrations of Neu5Ac. The proposed sensor showed desired optical properties including strong blue-fluorescence, excitation-dependent emission, multi-color imaging, and excellent fluorescence stability. These features are thoroughly constructive for investigating bioimaging potentials in vivo (zebrafish) models. The practicality of the sensor has verified, which obtained good spiking recovery results in serum (97–104.66%) and CSF samples (96.33–105.37%). In this regard, the developed APBA-ZnO-derived NSs are excellent alternatives for existing CDs or CQDs for Neu5Ac sensing. Overall, this research prospects the convenient utilization of fluorometric biosensors for the significant detection of indispensable neurological biomarkers.

Roopkumar Sangubotla: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jongsung Kim: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
